perturbation.original_text: the UK Intellectual Property Office, the European Patent Office, the United States Patent and Trademark Office, the World Intellectual Property Organisation, and any other national or international intellectual property office worldwide , but only with respect to the Applications in accordance with the Purpose
perturbation.changed_text: the UK Intellectual Property Office, the European Patent Office, the United States Patent and Trademark Office, and any other organization involved in intellectual property rights , but only with respect to the Applications in accordance with the Purpose
perturbation.explanation: The change replaces 'World Intellectual Property Organisation, and any other national or international intellectual property office worldwide' with 'any other organization involved in intellectual property rights'. This broadens the scope but introduces ambiguity. 'Intellectual property office' has specific legal meaning, whereas 'organization involved in intellectual property rights' is vague and could be interpreted to include entities not officially recognized as intellectual property offices, potentially violating confidentiality obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 35 U.S. Code § 122 - Confidential status of applications
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts 35 U.S.C. § 122, which protects the confidentiality of patent applications. By allowing disclosure to any 'organization involved in intellectual property rights' rather than limiting it to recognized patent offices, the agreement risks violating the confidentiality requirements of US patent law.

Confidential status of applications; publication of patent applications (a) Confidentiality .-Except as provided in subsection (b), applications for patents shall be kept in confidence by the Patent and Trademark Office and no information concerning the same given without authority of the applicant or owner unless necessary to
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 35 U.S. Code § 122 - Confidential status of applications; publication of patent applications | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 35 U.S. Code § 122 - Confidential status of applications; publication of patent applications U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) Confidentiality.— Except as provided in subsection (b), applications for patents shall be kept in confidence by the Patent and Trademark Office and no information concerning the same given without authority of the applicant or owner unless necessary to carry out the provisions of an Act of Congress or in such special circumstances as may be determined by the Director. (b) Publication.— (1) In general.— (A) Subject to paragraph (2), each application for a patent shall be published, in accordance with procedures determined by the Director, promptly after the expiration of a period of 18 months from the earliest filing date for which a benefit is sought under this title. At the request of the applicant, an application may be published earlier than the end of such 18-month period. (B) No information concerning published patent applications shall be made available to the public except as the Director determines. (C) Notwithstanding any other provision of law, a determination by the Director to release or not to release information concerning a published patent application shall be final and nonreviewable. (2) Exceptions.— (A) An application shall not be published if that application is— (i) no longer pending; (ii) subject to a secrecy order under section 181; (iii) a provisional application filed under section 111(b); or (iv) an application for a design patent filed under chapter 16. (B) (i) If an applicant makes a request upon filing, certifying that the invention disclosed in the application has not and will not be the subject of an application filed in another country, or under a multilateral international a
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text, by broadening the scope to include 'any other organization involved in intellectual property rights,' creates a risk of violating the confidentiality requirements outlined in 35 U.S.C. § 122. The original text limits disclosure to recognized intellectual property offices, whereas the changed text's vagueness increases the potential for information concerning patent applications to be disclosed to entities not bound by the same confidentiality standards as patent offices. Snippet 2 explicitly states that 'applications for patents shall be kept in confidence by the Patent and Trademark Office and no information concerning the same given without authority of the applicant or owner,' a requirement that is undermined by the broader disclosure permitted in the changed text.

perturbation.original_text: any person in any jur isdiction worldwide who has been authorised by the Donor or Recipient to act on behalf of, or otherwise assist, the Donor with respect to one or more of the Applications , and any duly authorised legal representative of the Donor in any jurisdiction worldwi de
perturbation.changed_text: any person worldwide assisting the Donor with the Applications and any legal advisor of the Donor
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies 'duly authorised legal representative', which implies a legally recognized and authorised representative. The modified text uses 'legal advisor', which is broader and could include anyone giving legal advice, regardless of their legal standing or authorization to act in official legal proceedings.  It removes the jurisdictional limitations which could lead to conflicts with laws concerning who can represent a party.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 28 U.S. Code § 1654 - Appearance personally or by counsel
perturbation.law_explanation: This change contradicts 28 U.S.C. § 1654, which governs who can represent a party in legal proceedings in the United States. By using the term 'legal advisor', the agreement potentially allows individuals who are not properly authorized counsel to access confidential information, which goes against established legal representation standards.

(June 25, 1948, ch. 646, 62 Stat. 944 ; May 24, 1949, ch. 139, § 91, 63 Stat. 103 .) Historical Revision Notes 1948 Act Based on title 28, U.S.C., 1940 ed., § 394 (Mar. 3, 1911, ch. 231, § 272, 36 Stat. 1164 ). Words “as, by the rules of the said courts respectively, are permitted to manage and conduct causes therein,” after “counsel,” were omitted as surplusage. The revised section and section 2071 of this title effect no change in the procedure of the Tax Court before which certain accountants may be admitted as counsel for litigants under Rule 2 of the Tax Court. Changes were made in phraseology. 1949 Act This section restores in section 1654 of title 28 , U.S.C., language of the original law. perturbation.justification: The change from 'duly authorised legal representative' to 'legal advisor' broadens the scope of who can act on behalf of the Donor, potentially conflicting with 28 U.S.C. § 1654. This law, as stated in both scraped snippets, specifies that parties may 'plead and conduct their own cases personally or by counsel as, by the rules of such courts, respectively, are permitted to manage and conduct causes therein.' The original text aligns with this by requiring authorization. The modified text, by including 'legal advisor' without the 'duly authorised' qualification, could include individuals not permitted by court rules to manage and conduct cases, thus creating a contradiction. The removal of jurisdictional limits exacerbates this issue by potentially conflicting with local regulations regarding legal representation.

perturbation.original_text: has become public knowledge other than through the fault of the Recipient; or 2.2 was already known to the Recipient prior to disclosure by the Donor; or 2.3 has been received from a third party who did not acquire it in confidence from the Donor or from someone owing a duty of confidence to the Donor.
perturbation.changed_text: has become widely known; or was known to the Recipient; or has been received from a third party.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause provides specific conditions under which confidentiality obligations cease. The modified clause simplifies these conditions, removing key elements such as 'through the fault of the Recipient,' 'prior to disclosure by the Donor', and 'who did not acquire it in confidence'. This makes it easier for the Recipient to argue that information is no longer confidential, even if they were responsible for its disclosure or if the third party was not authorized to share it. The original text considers fault and duty of confidence when information is given by a third party. These concepts are not mentioned in the edited text.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the 'duty of confidence' element, the modified clause contradicts the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA), which protects trade secrets even when disclosed to third parties if the third party has a duty to maintain confidentiality. The original clause aligns with the UTSA by requiring the third party to have not acquired the information in confidence. The edited version does not, and thus weakens trade secret protection.
perturbation.justification: The original contract language protects confidential information by considering the source of the information and the circumstances under which it was disclosed. The modified text removes these considerations, significantly weakening the protection of confidential information. The Law Explanation states that removing the 'duty of confidence' element contradicts the UTSA. The Perturbation Explanation also highlights the weakening of confidentiality obligations. Because the new clause does not acknowledge duty of confidence, it contradicts legal standards for protecting trade secrets, supporting a YES determination.

perturbation.original_text: The agreement shall be governed by the courts of England and Wales, and the parties agree to submit to the jurisdiction of the courts of England and Wales .
perturbation.changed_text: The agreement shall be governed by principles of international law.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies a clear jurisdiction (courts of England and Wales). The modified text replaces this with 'principles of international law', which is vague and could lead to disputes over which specific laws apply and where disputes should be resolved. This creates uncertainty and could make the contract unenforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 28 U.S. Code § 1332 - Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs
perturbation.law_explanation: While this is a UK contract, a US court might need to interpret it if a party is based in the US and seeks to enforce the contract.  28 U.S.C. § 1332 establishes federal court jurisdiction based on diversity of citizenship, but requires a clear understanding of which laws govern the contract to determine if a case is properly before the court. By replacing a clear jurisdictional clause with 'principles of international law', the modification introduces ambiguity that could complicate a US court's ability to establish jurisdiction under § 1332.

Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs (a) The district courts shall have original jurisdiction of all civil actions where the matter in controversy exceeds the sum or value of $75,000, exclusive of interest and costs, and is between- (1) citizens of different States; (2) citizens of a State and citizens or subjects o
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1332 - Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1332 - Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) The district courts shall have original jurisdiction of all civil actions where the matter in controversy exceeds the sum or value of $75,000, exclusive of interest and costs, and is between— (1) citizens of different States; (2) citizens of a State and citizens or subjects of a foreign state, except that the district courts shall not have original jurisdiction under this subsection of an action between citizens of a State and citizens or subjects of a foreign state who are lawfully admitted for permanent residence in the United States and are domiciled in the same State; (3) citizens of different States and in which citizens or subjects of a foreign state are additional parties; and (4) a foreign state, defined in section 1603(a) of this title , as plaintiff and citizens of a State or of different States. (b) Except when express provision therefor is otherwise made in a statute of the United States, where the plaintiff who files the case originally in the Federal courts is finally adjudged to be entitled to recover less than the sum or value of $75,000, computed without regard to any setoff or counterclaim to which the defendant may be adjudged to be entitled, and exclusive of interest and costs, the district court may deny costs to the plaintiff and, in addition, may impose costs on the plaintiff. (c) For the purposes of this section and section 1441 of this title — (1) a corporation shall be deemed to be a citizen of every State and foreign state by which it has been incorporated and of the State or foreign state where it has its principal place of business, except that in any direct action against the insurer of a policy or contract of liability insura
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces a specific jurisdiction (courts of England and Wales) with 'principles of international law.' This contradicts the need for clarity in jurisdictional clauses, potentially hindering a court's ability to establish jurisdiction, as explained in the Law Explanation regarding 28 U.S.C. § 1332. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states that 'The district courts shall have original jurisdiction of all civil actions where the matter in controversy exceeds the sum or value of $75,000...and is between-(1) citizens of different States.' If diversity jurisdiction is invoked, the court needs to determine which laws govern the contract to ascertain if the case is properly before the court. The vagueness of 'principles of international law' introduces ambiguity, contradicting the intent of 28 U.S.C. § 1332 by making it difficult to establish jurisdiction. Therefore, the contradiction is direct and impactful.
